sections.title: Host Hardening — OS Baseline & Attack Surface

items.title: Measure the host against a CIS Benchmark
items.description: Hosts should be configured against a recognized hardening baseline (CIS, DISA STIG) rather than vendor defaults, which are tuned for compatibility, not security.
items.how_to_find:
Run an automated CIS assessment:

```
# Linux
oscap xccdf eval --profile cis --results r.xml /usr/share/xccdf/ssg-<os>-ds.xml
# or Lynis
lynis audit system
```

Failed CIS controls = findings (prioritize by level).
items.how_to_fix: Apply a hardened CIS baseline via config management (Ansible/DSC), remediate failed controls, and enforce drift detection. Re-run the scan to confirm the score improves.

items.title: Disable unnecessary services and daemons
items.description: Every running service is attack surface. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate running/enabled services:

```
systemctl list-unit-files --state=enabled
ss -tulpn    # listening services + owning process
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable/mask services not required for the host's role (`systemctl disable --now <svc>`), remove their packages, and document the minimal service set. Re-check listeners.

items.title: Remove unnecessary Windows roles, features, and legacy protocols
items.description: Windows hosts often retain unneeded roles/features and legacy protocols like SMBv1 and LLMNR/NBT-NS that enable relay and poisoning attacks.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate roles/features and legacy protocols:

```
Get-WindowsFeature | ? Installed
Get-SmbServerConfiguration | select EnableSMB1Protocol
Get-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName SMB1Protocol
```

SMB1/legacy protocols enabled = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Remove unused roles/features, disable SMBv1 and other legacy protocols (`Disable-WindowsOptionalFeature -FeatureName SMB1Protocol`), and enforce via GPO/DSC. Re-verify they are removed.

items.title: Enforce login banners and disable insecure remote info disclosure
items.description: Pre-auth banners should warn against unauthorized access for legal coverage, and services should not leak OS/version detail unnecessarily.
items.how_to_find:
Check banners and version disclosure:

```
grep -RiE 'Banner|VersionAddendum' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
nc <host> 22    # observe banner/version
```
items.how_to_fix: Set legal login banners (`/etc/issue.net`, SSH `Banner`), suppress verbose version strings where possible, and disable unauthenticated info-disclosure services. Re-check banners/versions.

items.title: Enforce a strong password policy
items.description: Hosts should enforce minimum length, complexity/length-based strength, history, and age to resist guessing and reuse attacks.
items.how_to_find:
Review the password policy:

```
# Linux
grep -E 'minlen|dcredit|ucredit|difok' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
grep -E 'PASS_MAX_DAYS|PASS_MIN_LEN' /etc/login.defs
# Windows: net accounts  (or secpol.msc)
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce length (14+), complexity/banned-password lists, and history via `pwquality`/PAM or GPO. Prefer long passphrases + MFA. Re-check the policy.

items.title: Configure account lockout / failed-login throttling
items.description: Without lockout or rate limiting, accounts are open to unlimited online brute force and password spraying.
items.how_to_find:
Check lockout config:

```
# Linux: grep faillock /etc/pam.d/*  ;  grep -A3 pam_faillock /etc/security/faillock.conf
# Windows: net accounts | findstr /i lockout
```
items.how_to_fix: Configure a sane lockout threshold + reset window (pam_faillock / GPO), monitor for lockout storms, and combine with MFA. Re-verify lockout triggers.

items.title: Restrict and randomize local administrator credentials
items.description: Shared or reused local admin passwords enable lateral movement (pass-the-hash).
items.how_to_find:
Check for shared/static local admin passwords:

```
# Windows: confirm LAPS is deployed (Get-LapsADPassword ...) 
# Linux: check for shared root/sudo passwords across hosts
```
items.how_to_fix: Deploy LAPS (Windows) / unique per-host root credentials (Linux), rotate regularly, restrict who can read them, and avoid shared local admin passwords. Re-verify uniqueness.

items.title: Disable or secure guest and default accounts
items.description: Guest accounts and unchanged default/vendor accounts provide low-friction footholds and should be disabled or fully secured.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate default/guest accounts:

```
# Linux
awk -F: '($3>=0 && $3<1000){print $1}' /etc/passwd
# Windows: Get-LocalUser | ? Enabled ; check Guest/Administrator
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable/rename Guest and unused default accounts, remove default vendor credentials, and lock service accounts from interactive login. Re-audit accounts.

items.title: Identify world-writable files and directories
items.description: World-writable files and directories outside designated sticky-bit temp areas let any local user tamper with scripts, configs, or binaries used by privileged processes.
items.how_to_find:
Find world-writable paths (esp. without sticky bit):

```
find / -xdev -type f -perm -0002 2>/dev/null
find / -xdev -type d -perm -0002 ! -perm -1000 2>/dev/null
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove world-writable permissions from files/dirs (or add the sticky bit to shared dirs like `/tmp`), and least-privilege ownership. Re-scan to confirm.

items.title: Audit SUID/SGID binaries for privilege escalation
items.description: SUID/SGID binaries run with the file owner’s privileges.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate SUID/SGID binaries and check against GTFOBins:

```
find / -xdev \( -perm -4000 -o -perm -2000 \) -type f 2>/dev/null
```

Unexpected/GTFOBins-listed SUID binaries = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Remove the SUID/SGID bit from binaries that don't need it (`chmod u-s`), remove unnecessary tools, and monitor for new SUID files. Re-scan and verify against a known-good baseline.

items.title: Verify permissions on sensitive system files
items.description: Files like /etc/shadow, SSH host keys, and Windows SAM/SYSTEM hives must be readable only by privileged accounts to prevent credential theft.
items.how_to_find:
Check ownership/perms on key files:

```
ls -l /etc/shadow /etc/passwd /etc/sudoers /etc/ssh/sshd_config
find /etc -name '*.conf' -perm -0044 2>/dev/null
```

`/etc/shadow` readable by non-root = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Restore correct permissions (`/etc/shadow` 640/600 root, `/etc/sudoers` 440, SSH keys 600), enforce via config management, and monitor. Re-check permissions.

items.title: Harden mount options for non-system partitions
items.description: Mounting /tmp, /var, and removable media with noexec, nosuid, and nodev limits where attackers can drop and execute payloads or place setuid files.
items.how_to_find:
Check mount options on data/tmp partitions:

```
mount | grep -E '/tmp|/var|/home|/dev/shm'
grep -E '/tmp|/var|/home' /etc/fstab
```

Missing `noexec,nosuid,nodev` on `/tmp`, `/dev/shm` = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Add `noexec,nosuid,nodev` to non-system partitions (`/tmp`, `/var/tmp`, `/dev/shm`, `/home` where feasible) in `/etc/fstab`, and remount. Re-check mount options.

sections.title: Patch & Vulnerability Management — Patch Posture & EOL Software

items.title: Identify missing security patches
items.description: Unpatched hosts are exposed to known, weaponized CVEs.
items.how_to_find:
Check for pending security updates:

```
# Debian/Ubuntu
apt-get -s upgrade | grep -i security
# RHEL
dnf updateinfo list security
# Windows: Get-HotFix ; or WSUS/SCCM/Defender report
```
items.how_to_fix: Apply outstanding security updates promptly, automate patching with maintenance windows, and track compliance. Re-scan after patching.

items.title: Detect end-of-life OS and software
items.description: EOL operating systems and applications no longer receive security fixes, leaving permanent exposure to newly disclosed flaws.
items.how_to_find:
Identify EOL OS/software (no more security fixes):

```
cat /etc/os-release ; hostnamectl    # compare to vendor EOL dates
# check key app versions against EOL calendars
```
items.how_to_fix: Upgrade or replace EOL OS/software, isolate anything that can't be upgraded (segmentation + compensating controls), and track lifecycle. Re-verify supported versions.

items.title: Assess third-party and application patch coverage
items.description: Patch programs often cover the OS but miss third-party software (browsers, runtimes, agents), which is a common initial-access vector.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate installed third-party software and versions vs known CVEs:

```
# Linux: dpkg -l / rpm -qa ; feed to a vuln scanner
# Windows: winget list / registry uninstall keys
# run a scanner: nessus / openvas / grype (containers)
```
items.how_to_fix: Include third-party apps in patch management, remove unused software, and remediate high/critical CVEs. Re-scan.

items.title: Validate patch cadence and reboot compliance
items.description: Patches that are installed but not applied (pending reboot) or deployed on a slow cadence leave a window of exposure even on "managed" hosts.
items.how_to_find:
Check last patch/reboot to spot stale hosts:

```
uptime -p ; ls -l /var/log/dpkg.log
# Windows: (gcim Win32_OperatingSystem).LastBootUpTime ; Get-HotFix | sort InstalledOn
```

Pending-reboot state means patches aren't fully applied.
items.how_to_fix: Define + enforce a patch cadence with required reboots (maintenance windows), monitor pending-reboot state, and report compliance. Re-verify hosts are current.

sections.title: Patch & Vulnerability Management — Kernel & Local Privesc CVEs

items.title: Check kernel version against known local-privesc CVEs
items.description: Outdated Linux kernels are vulnerable to well-known local privilege escalation exploits (Dirty COW, Dirty Pipe, OverlayFS, PwnKit).
items.how_to_find:
Compare kernel version to known privesc CVEs:

```
uname -r
# run linux-exploit-suggester or LinPEAS
./linux-exploit-suggester.sh
```
items.how_to_fix: Patch/upgrade the kernel, reboot to activate, and enable exploit mitigations. Isolate hosts that can't be patched. Re-run the suggester after patching.

items.title: Test for PwnKit / vulnerable setuid helpers (CVE-2021-4034)
items.description: PwnKit in polkit’s pkexec gives any local user instant root on a huge range of Linux distributions, and similar setuid helper bugs recur.
items.how_to_find:
Check pkexec/polkit and other setuid helpers:

```
pkexec --version
dpkg -l policykit-1 2>/dev/null || rpm -q polkit
```

Vulnerable versions = critical local root.
items.how_to_fix: Patch polkit/pkexec (and other vulnerable helpers), or as a stopgap remove the SUID bit from `pkexec`. Re-verify the version is fixed.

items.title: Check Windows for known local-privesc CVEs
items.description: Windows hosts missing key updates are exposed to local privilege escalation chains (PrintNightmare, HiveNightmare/SeriousSAM, and various LPE CVEs).
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate missing patches tied to privesc:

```
# WinPEAS, or:
Get-HotFix | sort InstalledOn
# compare against known privesc CVEs (PrintNightmare, HiveNightmare, etc.)
```
items.how_to_fix: Apply the relevant security updates, verify KB coverage, and enable mitigations. Re-scan with WinPEAS/vuln scanner after patching.

items.title: Restrict loadable kernel modules and exploit primitives
items.description: Unrestricted module loading and exposed kernel features (unprivileged user namespaces, BPF) expand the local kernel attack surface used by privesc exploits.
items.how_to_find:
Check module-loading and hardening sysctls:

```
sysctl kernel.modules_disabled kernel.kptr_restrict kernel.dmesg_restrict kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled
```
items.how_to_fix: Blacklist unused modules, set hardening sysctls (`kptr_restrict=2`, `dmesg_restrict=1`, `unprivileged_bpf_disabled=1`, disable unused filesystems), and lock down module loading. Re-verify sysctls.

sections.title: Service Configuration — Exposed Services & Default Configs

items.title: Enumerate and justify all listening services
items.description: Each open port is a potential entry point.
items.how_to_find:
List listeners and map to a justification:

```
ss -tulpn
nmap -sS -sV <host>   # external view
```
items.how_to_fix: Close/firewall services without a business justification, bind to localhost where external access isn't needed, and document the approved listener set. Re-scan.

items.title: Test for default and weak service credentials
items.description: Databases, admin panels, and middleware shipped with default credentials are a leading cause of compromise and lateral movement.
items.how_to_find:
Test services for default/weak creds:

```
nmap --script <svc>-brute -p <port> <host>
hydra -C defaults.txt <proto>://<host>
```
items.how_to_fix: Change all default credentials, enforce strong passwords + MFA where supported, and disable unused accounts. Rotate exposed creds. Re-test.

items.title: Identify cleartext and legacy protocol exposure
items.description: Services like FTP, Telnet, HTTP, and unencrypted SMTP/LDAP expose credentials and data to network interception.
items.how_to_find:
Find cleartext/legacy protocols (telnet, FTP, HTTP, SNMPv1/2c, SMBv1):

```
nmap -sV -p 21,23,80,161,445 <host>
```
items.how_to_fix: Replace with encrypted equivalents (SSH/SFTP/HTTPS/SNMPv3), disable legacy protocols, and firewall what remains. Re-scan.

items.title: Verify services run under least-privilege accounts
items.description: Network-facing services running as root/SYSTEM turn any service exploit into full host compromise; they should run as dedicated low-privilege accounts.
items.how_to_find:
Check what services run as root/SYSTEM:

```
ps -eo user,comm | sort -u | grep '^root'
# Windows: Get-CimInstance Win32_Service | ? {$_.StartName -match 'LocalSystem'}
```
items.how_to_fix: Run services under dedicated low-privilege accounts (or gMSA on Windows), drop capabilities, and use systemd sandboxing (`ProtectSystem`, `NoNewPrivileges`). Re-audit service accounts.

items.title: Harden SSH server configuration
items.description: Default sshd settings often permit root login, password auth, and weak access control, making the primary admin channel an easy target.
items.how_to_find:
Review sshd config:

```
sshd -T | grep -iE 'permitrootlogin|passwordauthentication|permitemptypasswords|x11forwarding|maxauthtries'
```

Root login / password auth / empty passwords = findings.
items.how_to_fix: Set `PermitRootLogin no`, `PasswordAuthentication no` (key/cert + MFA), `PermitEmptyPasswords no`, limit `MaxAuthTries`, disable unused forwarding, and restrict users. Reload sshd and re-check.

items.title: Disable weak SSH/TLS ciphers, MACs, and key exchanges
items.description: Legacy ciphers and key-exchange algorithms (CBC modes, SHA-1 MACs, weak DH groups) undermine the confidentiality of the management channel.
items.how_to_find:
Audit negotiated algorithms:

```
ssh-audit <host>
sshd -T | grep -iE 'ciphers|macs|kexalgorithms'
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict to strong ciphers/MACs/KEX (e.g. chacha20/aes-gcm, hmac-sha2-*-etm, curve25519), remove CBC/SHA1/weak KEX, and re-run ssh-audit to confirm.

items.title: Restrict exposure of remote management interfaces
items.description: SSH (22) and RDP (3389) reachable from the internet are prime targets for brute force, credential stuffing, and exploit chains like BlueKeep.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether SSH/RDP/WinRM are internet-exposed:

```
nmap -p 22,3389,5985,5986 <external-ip>
ss -tulpn | grep -E ':22|:3389|:5985'
```

Management ports open to the internet = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Restrict management access to VPN/bastion/jump hosts + specific source IPs, firewall the ports from the internet, and require MFA. Re-scan externally.

items.title: Enforce NLA and multi-factor on remote access
items.description: RDP without Network Level Authentication, and SSH/RDP without MFA, allow pre-auth interaction and single-factor compromise of admin access.
items.how_to_find:
Check RDP NLA and MFA:

```
# Windows: (Get-CimInstance -Namespace root/cimv2/TerminalServices -ClassName Win32_TSGeneralSetting).UserAuthenticationRequired
```

NLA disabled / no MFA on remote access = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Require NLA for RDP, enforce MFA on all remote access (RDP gateway/VPN/SSH), and restrict via conditional access. Re-verify NLA + MFA.

items.title: Find privileged cron/tasks calling writable scripts
items.description: A root cron job or SYSTEM scheduled task that executes a user-writable script or binary is a direct privilege escalation path.
items.how_to_find:
Find root cron jobs invoking writable scripts:

```
cat /etc/crontab /etc/cron.d/* ; ls -l /etc/cron.*/
# check each referenced script's permissions (writable by non-root?)
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict cron scripts to root-owned, non-writable paths, remove world/group-writable perms, and least-privilege the jobs. Re-check script permissions.

items.title: Detect wildcard and PATH abuse in scheduled jobs
items.description: Cron jobs using wildcards (e.g. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find:
Look for wildcards (e.g. `tar *`) and relative binaries in jobs:

```
grep -REn '\*|\.\./|^[^/]' /etc/cron* /var/spool/cron/ 2>/dev/null
```

Wildcard injection (tar/rsync) and relative PATH = privesc.
items.how_to_fix: Use absolute paths for binaries/args, avoid shell wildcards on attacker-influenced dirs (use `--` and explicit file lists), and set a safe PATH in the job. Re-review jobs.

items.title: Review scheduled-task definition and binary permissions
items.description: On Windows, scheduled tasks whose XML definition or target executable can be modified by low-privilege users let attackers escalate to the task’s run-as identity.
items.how_to_find:
On Windows, review tasks and their target binaries:

```
Get-ScheduledTask | ? {$_.Principal.RunLevel -eq 'Highest'} 
schtasks /query /fo LIST /v
# check ACLs on each Action's binary
```
items.how_to_fix: Ensure task binaries/scripts are in non-writable, admin-owned paths, run tasks with least privilege, and restrict task modification. Re-audit task binary ACLs.

items.title: Restrict who can create scheduled tasks and cron jobs
items.description: Allowing arbitrary users to register tasks/cron entries enables persistence and privilege abuse; job creation should be governed.
items.how_to_find:
Check who may create jobs:

```
cat /etc/cron.allow /etc/cron.deny 2>/dev/null
# Windows: membership that can register tasks
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict cron/task creation to admins (`cron.allow`), monitor task/cron creation events, and least-privilege users. Re-verify allow lists.

items.title: Audit sudo rules for privilege escalation
items.description: Overly broad sudo rules, NOPASSWD entries, and binaries allowed via sudo that have GTFOBins escape vectors lead directly to root.
items.how_to_find:
Review sudo rights (check against GTFOBins):

```
sudo -l
cat /etc/sudoers /etc/sudoers.d/* 2>/dev/null
```

`NOPASSWD` on GTFOBins binaries or `ALL` = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Least-privilege sudo (specific commands, no wildcards/editors/interpreters that GTFOBins abuses), avoid broad `NOPASSWD`, use `secure_path`, and log sudo. Re-audit rules.

items.title: Enumerate dangerous file capabilities
items.description: Linux capabilities (e.g. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find:
Find binaries with privesc-worthy capabilities:

```
getcap -r / 2>/dev/null
```

`cap_setuid`, `cap_dac_override`, etc. on shells/interpreters = privesc.
items.how_to_fix: Remove unnecessary capabilities (`setcap -r <bin>`), avoid granting powerful caps to interpreters, and prefer least privilege. Re-scan capabilities.

items.title: Check for PATH and library hijacking
items.description: Privileged scripts that call binaries by relative name, or that load libraries from writable locations, can be hijacked to run attacker code as root.
items.how_to_find:
Look for writable dirs in root's PATH and hijackable libraries:

```
echo $PATH ; ls -ld $(echo $PATH | tr ':' ' ')
find / -name '*.so*' -perm -0002 2>/dev/null   # world-writable libs
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove writable/relative entries from privileged PATHs, fix library permissions/ownership, and avoid `LD_PRELOAD`/`LD_LIBRARY_PATH` in privileged contexts. Re-check.

items.title: Test NFS exports for no_root_squash abuse
items.description: NFS shares exported with no_root_squash let a remote root user create setuid-root binaries on the share, yielding root on the server.
items.how_to_find:
Check exports for `no_root_squash` (lets a remote root write SUID files):

```
cat /etc/exports ; showmount -e <host>
```

`no_root_squash` = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Enable `root_squash` (default) on exports, restrict exports to specific hosts, avoid exporting sensitive paths, and use Kerberos (sec=krb5). Re-check exports.

items.title: Find unquoted service paths
items.description: A service with an unquoted path containing spaces can be hijacked by placing a malicious executable in an earlier path segment, escalating to the service account.
items.how_to_find:
Find services with unquoted paths containing spaces:

```
wmic service get name,pathname,startmode | findstr /i "auto"
# unquoted paths with spaces (and no leading quote) are vulnerable; or use WinPEAS
```
items.how_to_fix: Quote all service `ImagePath` values, ensure parent dirs aren't writable by non-admins, and audit service configs. Re-check for unquoted paths.

items.title: Audit weak service and binary permissions
items.description: Services whose configuration (SERVICE_CHANGE_CONFIG) or binary is writable by low-privilege users allow replacing the executable to run as SYSTEM.
items.how_to_find:
Check for services/binaries modifiable by non-admins:

```
accesschk.exe -uwcqv "Users" *    # writable services
accesschk.exe -quvw "Users" "C:\Program Files\..."
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove write/modify rights for non-admins on service configs and binaries, and least-privilege service accounts. Re-audit ACLs.

items.title: Check for abusable token privileges
items.description: Privileges like SeImpersonatePrivilege, SeAssignPrimaryToken, and SeBackupPrivilege enable token-impersonation (Potato) and credential-theft escalations to SYSTEM.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate dangerous privileges:

```
whoami /priv
# look for SeImpersonate, SeAssignPrimaryToken, SeBackup, SeRestore, SeDebug
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove unnecessary privileges from service/user accounts (esp. SeImpersonate/SeDebug), patch to prevent Potato attacks, and monitor. Re-audit privileges.

items.title: Test AlwaysInstallElevated policy
items.description: When AlwaysInstallElevated is enabled for both HKLM and HKCU, any user can install an MSI that runs as SYSTEM — a one-step privilege escalation.
items.how_to_find:
Check the AlwaysInstallElevated MSI policy (installs MSIs as SYSTEM):

```
reg query HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated
reg query HKCU\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated
```

Both = 1 is critical local privesc.
items.how_to_fix: Disable AlwaysInstallElevated (set both keys to 0 / remove via GPO), and restrict who can install software. Re-verify the policy is off.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK — Bypass User Account Control (T1548.002)

sections.title: Privilege & Access — Secrets on the Host

items.title: Search the filesystem for stored credentials
items.description: Plaintext passwords, API keys, and private keys in config files, scripts, and backups give attackers ready-made credentials for escalation or lateral movement.
items.how_to_find:
Grep the filesystem for secrets:

```
grep -REn 'password|secret|api[_-]?key|-----BEGIN' /etc /opt /home /var/www 2>/dev/null
# Windows: findstr /si password *.xml *.ini *.config C:\
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove stored credentials, move secrets to a vault (Vault/DPAPI/Keychain), restrict file permissions, and rotate exposed values. Re-scan.

items.title: Inspect shell and command history for secrets
items.description: Shell history (.bash_history, PowerShell history/transcripts) frequently captures passwords passed on the command line.
items.how_to_find:
Check history files:

```
cat ~/.bash_history ~/.zsh_history ~/.mysql_history 2>/dev/null | grep -iE 'pass|token|key'
# Windows: (Get-PSReadlineOption).HistorySavePath
```
items.how_to_fix: Clear secrets from history, avoid passing secrets on the command line (use prompts/env/files with restricted perms), configure `HISTIGNORE`, and rotate exposed creds. Re-check history.

items.title: Check environment variables and process listings for secrets
items.description: Secrets injected as environment variables can leak through /proc, process listings, and crash dumps to other local users.
items.how_to_find:
Look for secrets in env/process args:

```
cat /proc/*/environ 2>/dev/null | tr '\0' '\n' | grep -iE 'pass|token|key'
ps auxww | grep -iE 'password=|token='
```
items.how_to_fix: Don't pass secrets via env/CLI args (visible to other users); use files with strict perms or a secrets manager, and rotate exposed values. Re-check.

items.title: Hunt for credentials in registry, vaults, and config stores
items.description: Windows hosts store credentials in autologon registry keys, the Credential Manager, unattend/sysprep files, and SAM/LSA secrets that attackers routinely harvest.
items.how_to_find:
Search Windows credential stores/registry:

```
reg query HKLM /f password /t REG_SZ /s
cmdkey /list ; vaultcmd /listcreds:"Windows Credentials"
# check autologon: winlogon DefaultPassword
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove stored plaintext creds (esp. autologon `DefaultPassword`), use Credential Manager/DPAPI/LAPS properly, restrict access, and rotate exposed secrets. Re-scan.

items.title: Confirm audit/event logging is enabled with adequate coverage
items.description: Without auditing of authentication, privilege use, and configuration changes, incidents cannot be detected or reconstructed.
items.how_to_find:
Check auditing config:

```
# Linux: auditctl -l ; systemctl status auditd
# Windows: auditpol /get /category:*
```

Key categories (logon, privilege use, process creation, object access) disabled = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Enable auditd/Advanced Audit Policy with coverage for authentication, privilege use, process creation, and sensitive object access; deploy a baseline audit ruleset via config management. Re-verify coverage.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK — Impair Defenses: Disable Logging (T1562.002)

items.title: Verify logs are forwarded to central, tamper-resistant storage
items.description: Logs kept only locally can be wiped by an attacker who compromises the host; central forwarding preserves evidence and enables correlation.
items.how_to_find:
Check for log forwarding:

```
grep -RiE 'rsyslog|syslog-ng|@@' /etc/rsyslog.conf /etc/rsyslog.d/ 2>/dev/null
# Windows: WEF/WEC or agent status
```
items.how_to_fix: Forward logs to a central SIEM/collector over TLS, ensure the pipeline is reliable and access-controlled, and set retention. Re-verify events arrive centrally.

items.title: Protect logs from tampering and clearing
items.description: Attackers commonly clear or disable logs to cover tracks; local log files and event logs must be access-controlled and clear events alerted on.
items.how_to_find:
Check local log protections and clearing:

```
ls -l /var/log ; lsattr /var/log/*.log 2>/dev/null
# Windows: audit for event-log clear (Event ID 1102)
```
items.how_to_fix: Ship logs off-host immediately (so local clearing doesn't lose data), restrict log file permissions, use append-only/immutable where possible, and alert on log clears (1102). Re-verify.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK — Indicator Removal: Clear Logs (T1070)

items.title: Enable command-line and process-execution logging
items.description: Capturing process creation with command lines (Sysmon / auditd execve, Windows 4688 with command line) is essential for detecting hands-on-keyboard attacks.
items.how_to_find:
Check process/command-line auditing:

```
# Linux: auditctl -l | grep execve ; or auditd execve rules
# Windows: auditpol /get /subcategory:"Process Creation" ; check cmdline logging (4688) + Sysmon
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable process-creation logging with command line (Windows 4688 + `ProcessCreationIncludeCmdLine`, Sysmon; Linux auditd execve rules), and forward centrally. Re-verify events include command lines.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK — Command and Scripting Interpreter (T1059)

sections.title: Logging & Monitoring — Time Sync & Log Integrity

items.title: Verify reliable, authenticated time synchronization
items.description: Accurate time is foundational to correlating logs across hosts and to Kerberos; unsynced clocks break investigations and authentication.
items.how_to_find:
Check time sync status:

```
timedatectl status ; chronyc sources 2>/dev/null || ntpq -p
# Windows: w32tm /query /status
```

Unsynced/large offset undermines log correlation + Kerberos.
items.how_to_fix: Sync all hosts to reliable NTP sources (ideally authenticated NTS/symmetric key), monitor drift, and alert on sync failure. Re-verify offsets are small.

items.title: Confirm consistent time zone and timestamp format across hosts
items.description: Inconsistent time zones or local-time logging across a fleet makes cross-host correlation error-prone during incident response.
items.how_to_find:
Check timezone/timestamp consistency:

```
timedatectl | grep 'Time zone'
```

Mixed timezones/formats hinder cross-host correlation.
items.how_to_fix: Standardize on UTC (or one documented zone) and ISO-8601/RFC3339 timestamps across all hosts and logs, enforced via config management. Re-verify consistency.

items.title: Validate log integrity controls and retention
items.description: Integrity controls (append-only storage, hashing, write-once retention) ensure logs are trustworthy evidence and meet retention requirements.
items.how_to_find:
Check retention + integrity controls:

```
grep -RiE 'rotate|maxage|maxsize' /etc/logrotate.conf /etc/logrotate.d/ 2>/dev/null
```
items.how_to_fix: Set retention to meet compliance, protect archived logs (integrity hashing/WORM/immutable storage), restrict access, and verify restores. Re-verify retention + integrity.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK — Indicator Removal: Clear Logs (T1070)
